title: Wet-felted lampshade cover
subtitle: A flat merino panel pre-shrunk and shaped to wrap a cylindrical lampshade frame
excerpt: Wet-felt a flat merino panel that shrinks to approximately 30 cm tall by 90 cm circumference, then wrap it over a plain cylindrical lampshade frame. Natural wool colours only; no mordanting required. Use with a cool LED bulb only: wool should never be used near incandescent or halogen heat.
sourceNotes: Observation-led; wet-felted lampshade covers are a recognised intermediate-level wet-felting project, documented in fibre-arts guild teaching materials.

glossaryTerms.definition: Combed and drawn wool fibres aligned in a continuous rope or strip, ready for spinning or felting. In wet felting, wisps are pulled from roving and laid in overlapping layers before wetting and agitating.

The stage of wet felting in which the partially felted panel is subjected to vigorous rolling, throwing, or rubbing to cause maximum shrinkage and increase the density of the fabric. Fulling follows the initial felting stage once the surface fibres have matted and the panel holds together without the net. It is during fulling that the fabric reaches its final size and firmness.

content.text: This cover is made as a flat panel from merino

that is felted and wrapped around a cylindrical lampshade frame. The overlap is glued or stitched at the back. The pre-shrink working panel is 42 cm tall by 130 cm wide. This shrinks roughly 30 percent to the finished 30 cm x 90 cm dimensions. A standard 30 cm diameter drum shade has a circumference of approximately 94 cm, so the finished panel fits with a small overlap at the join. Measure your own shade before starting. Multiply the finished height by 1.45 and the finished circumference by 1.45 to get your working dimensions.
Use only with a cool LED bulb. Wool is flammable and must not be placed near incandescent or halogen heat sources.

attrs.name: Merino roving, natural ecru or undyed grey, 250 g
Hot water, around 50 to 55 degrees C
Felting mat, at least 145 x 45 cm (or two mats placed end to end)
Felting net, at least 150 x 50 cm
Cylindrical drum lampshade frame, 30 cm diameter x 30 cm tall
Fabric glue or needle and thread, for securing the back seam

content.text: Lay the felting mat on a long table or on the floor. Pull thin wisps from the merino roving and lay them horizontally across the 42 x 130 cm working area in two full horizontal layers, working back and forth across the full width. Add two further layers at 90 degrees to the first pair. Keep each layer thin and even: about 2 mm depth per layer. At the short ends (the top and bottom edges of the finished shade), fold the overhanging wisps back on themselves so the edge is a double thickness; this prevents a thin, weak top and bottom edge on the finished panel. Use the tape measure periodically to maintain the correct working dimensions.

Lay the net over the full panel. Mix the olive-oil soap into the hot water and pour slowly along the full length and width of the panel, distributing water evenly. Press down with flat palms in a steady rhythm from one end to the other, saturating the roving without displacing layers. Work the edges separately: they need as much water as the centre. The panel will darken uniformly when fully wet. Tip the mat slightly to drain any visible pooling at the edges.

Rub through the net with flat palms in firm, even circles across the entire surface. Work systematically from one end of the panel to the other and back, repeating the pass 4 to 5 times. After 5 minutes of rubbing, test a corner: lift the net and try to pinch the surface fibres. They should resist being lifted as a group rather than pulling away individually. If fibres still pull away easily, replace the net and continue rubbing. Once the surface is holding, remove the net and rub the surface directly with soapy palms for a further 4 minutes.

Move to the bamboo rolling mat. Lay the panel on the mat with the long axis parallel to the mat's roll direction. Roll up firmly and roll back and forth on the table for 4 minutes with firm palm pressure. Unroll, rotate the panel 90 degrees, and roll again for 4 minutes. Measure after every two passes: note both the height and the width separately. You are aiming for 30 cm height and 90 cm width. Shock the panel with cold water after every third rolling pass to accelerate
. Return to hot soapy water immediately after the cold shock. Expect 8 to 12 rolling passes in total before reaching the finished dimensions.

Once the panel has reached 30 x 90 cm, rinse thoroughly in lukewarm water until the water runs clear. While the panel is still damp, check the long edges (top and bottom of the finished shade) for waviness. Trim with sharp scissors to a clean straight line if needed. Roll in a tea towel and press firmly; do not wring. Lay flat on a clean towel and pull to square. Allow to dry completely before wrapping onto the frame: a damp wool panel will stretch when handled and may not hold the shape it dries to on the frame.

Wrap the dry panel around the lampshade frame, positioning the join at the back. The panel should meet at the back with approximately 2 cm overlap. Secure the overlap with a thin line of fabric glue along the inner face of the overlapping edge, or whipstitch the join with a matching thread using a blunt darning needle. The top and bottom edges of the panel should sit just at the top and bottom rings of the frame; they do not need to be turned or stitched, as the felt edge is naturally stable. If the panel is slightly longer than the frame, the excess can be trimmed with scissors once glued in place.

attrs.problem: The panel has reached the correct height but is still 110 cm wide
attrs.cause: Rolling was performed mainly along the horizontal axis, so the panel shrank more in height than in width
attrs.fix: Rotate 90 degrees between every rolling pass. Continue rolling with the long dimension as the roll axis until both measurements are at the planned size.

attrs.problem: The top and bottom edges are wavy rather than straight
attrs.cause: The edge wisps were not folded back during layout, leaving the boundary fibres unsupported; or the panel was not pulled square while still damp
attrs.fix: Trim the edge to a clean straight line with sharp scissors while the panel is damp. Re-wet briefly if the panel has already dried; damp felt trims cleaner than dry.

attrs.problem: The surface has a patchy, thin area in the centre of the panel
attrs.cause: The roving layers were not even: the centre was laid thinner than the edges, so the centre felted faster and became over-fulled while the edges caught up
attrs.fix: Lay roving in thin, consistent wisps and check thickness across the full panel before wetting. If the thin patch is already felted, it can be patched after drying by applying a wisp of matching roving and needle-felting it to the surface.
